paper_title: RubiCap: Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Dense Image Captioning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Dense image captioning is critical for cross-modal alignment in vision-language pretraining and text-to-image generation, but scaling expert-quality annotations is prohibitively expensive. While synthetic captioning via strong vision-language models (VLMs) is a practical alternative, supervised distillation often yields limited output diversity and weak generalization. Reinforcement learning (RL) could overcome these limitations, but its successes have so far been concentrated in verifiable domains that rely on deterministic checkers -- a luxury not available in open-ended captioning. We address this bottleneck with RubiCap, a novel RL framework that derives fine-grained, sample-specific reward signals from LLM-written rubrics. RubiCap first assembles a diverse committee of candidate captions, then employs an LLM rubric writer to extract consensus strengths and diagnose deficiencies in the current policy. These insights are converted into explicit evaluation criteria, enabling an LLM judge to decompose holistic quality assessment and replace coarse scalar rewards with structured, multi-faceted evaluations. Across extensive benchmarks, RubiCap achieves the highest win rates on CapArena, outperforming supervised distillation, prior RL methods, human-expert annotations, and GPT-4V-augmented outputs. On CaptionQA, it demonstrates superior word efficiency: our 7B model matches Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct, and our 3B model surpasses its 7B counterpart. Remarkably, using the compact RubiCap-3B as a captioner produces stronger pretrained VLMs than those trained on captions from proprietary models.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Dense image captioning shifts traditional vision-language tasks from providing global scene summaries (Chen et al., 2015; Vinyals et al., 2015; Sharma et al., 2018) to generating fine-grained, region-level descriptions of objects, attributes, and spatial relationships (Chen et al., 2020; Park and Paik, 2023; Zhou et al., 2024; Deitke et al., 2025). This richer form of visual understanding has become core building block for crossmodal alignment in visual-language pretraining (Radford et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024), visual instruction tuning (Liu et al., 2023), and controllable text-to-image generation (Kim et al., 2023). However, scaling dense captioning without sacrificing quality remains challenging. Manual annotation demands expert-level visual perception and precise language grounding, combination that is prohibitively expensive at the scale required by frontier models. Recent efforts therefore turn to using stronger visionlanguage models (VLMs) to generate synthetic captions, which are usually then distilled into smaller, specialized captioners through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) (Yang et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024; Singla et al., 2024). However, SFT comes with pitfalls: (i) it tends to collapse linguistic diversity through memorization (Chu et al., 2025) (e.g., replicating teachers narrative style rather than improving visual understanding), (ii) induces severe catastrophic forgetting of pretrained capabilities (McCloskey and Cohen, 1989; Lai et al., 2025; Shenfeld et al., 2025), and (iii) degrades when teacher and student distributions mismatch (Gerstgrasser et al., 2024). These shortcomings motivate shift toward feedback-driven optimization, specifically through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) (Team et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2025). RLVR has driven major advances in mathematical reasoning (Shao et al., 2024) and code generation (Luo et al., 2025; Liu and 1 Zhang, 2025), where solutions are hard to produce but trivial to verify. Dense captioning, however, does not naturally admit such verification: caption quality is inherently open-ended, subjective, and contextdependent, with no deterministic verifier available. This verification bottleneck remains the key obstacle to bringing RL into dense captioning. Prior attempts to provide RL rewards primarily fall into two categories, each with drawbacks that stymie their adoption: Lexical NLP Metrics: Metrics such as CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015), and ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004) measure n-gram overlap against reference captions. While computationally efficient, they are strictly reference-bound and are insensitive to semantic equivalence or compositional variation, rewarding lexical similarity rather than descriptive accuracy. VLM-as-a-Judge: Alternatively, frontier VLMs are increasingly used as holistic evaluators that assign quality ratings (Lee et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024; Lee et al., 2024). However, these scores are often coarse and opaque; they oversimplify complex quality dimensions into single scalar, providing minimal diagnostic insight into specific failures. We address this verification bottleneck by deriving fine-grained, sample-specific evaluation criteria, transforming an inherently subjective judgment into structured, multi-faceted assessment. Specifically, we propose RubiCap, novel RL framework that decomposes caption quality using suite of rubrics and leverages them to produce reliable RL rewards. RubiCap begins with committee of diverse VLMs producing candidate descriptions for each image. An LLM rubric writer then extracts the consensus while identifying deficiencies in the students current policy. These targeted gaps are transformed into interpretable, easy-tocheck rules that an LLM judge can readily apply to caption assessment, yielding more precise feedback that goes far beyond what global quality score can offer. This design offers several key advantages. First, it opens multi-dimensional evaluation: rubrics can simultaneously check object presence, attribute correctness, spatial reasoning, and hallucination. Second, rubric creation scales naturally : for human annotators, producing coherent evaluation criteria is cognitively demanding, even harder than writing captions themselves, whereas an LLM rubric writer synthesizes them reliably at scale. Finally, rubrics are steerable by design: by directing the rubric writer toward specific weaknesses, the reward signal can be precisely aimed at the areas most in need of improvement. We evaluate RubiCap extensively across diverse captioning benchmarks. On CapArena (Cheng et al., 2025), judged by GPT-4.1 (OpenAI, 2024), our 7B model achieves the highest win rates across all compared methods, notably outperforming human-expert annotations and proprietary model outputs. In blind ranking evaluation, RubiCap-7B earns the highest proportion of rank-1 assignments among all modelsincluding 72B and 32B frontiersachieving the lowest hallucination penalty and strongest accuracy. Beyond captioning quality, RubiCap exhibits improved word efficiency on CaptionQA (Yang et al., 2025) by prioritizing salient content; specifically, RubiCap-3B models outperform 7B base models, while our 7B models can match 32B-scale frontiers. Additionally, RubiCap-trained models preserve pretrained capabilities across 10 VLM benchmarks, substantially mitigating severe forgetting observed in supervised distillation. Finally, we show that compact RubiCap-3B captioner can serve as high-quality data source for VLM pretraining, resulting in stronger pretrained models than those built on GPT-4V captions. We summarize our contributions as follows: We identify the verification bottleneck in RL for dense captioning and address it through synthetic, samplespecific rubrics for fine-grained, reliable reward signals. We propose an automated rubric synthesis pipeline that leverages diverse model consensus and targeted deficiency analysis to decompose holistic evaluation into multi-faceted quality checks. We conduct extensive experiments across six evaluation axes, demonstrating that RubiCap consistently delivers the largest gains on base models and outperforms diverse baselines in both caption quality and word efficiency. We show that RubiCap-7B outperforms 72B and 32B frontier models in blind ranking evaluations, earning the highest proportion of rank-1 assignments while achieving the lowest hallucination penalty and strongest accuracy. 2 We demonstrate that RubiCap-trained models serve as stronger captioners than proprietary systems, yielding better pretrained VLMs at scale.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: Our work sits at the intersection of two research threads: dense image captioning and reinforcement learning for vision-language models. Dense Image Captioning. Early image captioning research was driven by datasets such as MS COCO (Chen et al., 2015) and Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2015), where human annotators provided concise, scene-level summaries. Recent efforts have shifted toward dense captioning, which requires both global description and the localization of multiple salient regions. However, collecting such fine-grained annotations at scale is substantially more demanding, motivating researchers to explore synthetic alternatives. One line of research reuses web-crawled alt-text, refining it with LLMs for greater clarity and descriptiveness (Fan et al., 2023; Lai et al., 2024; Singla et al., 2024). Other methods prompt strong VLMs directly (Chen et al., 2024), fuses outputs from multiple VLMs (Yu et al., 2024), or enrich captions with predictions from external vision experts (Li et al., 2024, 2025; Zhu et al., 2025). Most of these approaches rely on SFT to distill synthetic captions into specialized captioner. RubiCap takes different path: instead of imitating fixed teacher, it trains via RL with rewards derived from fine-grained, sample-specific rubricsletting the model discover better captions rather than merely reproducing existing ones. Reinforcement Learning in Visual Language Models. Reinforcement learning has recently driven major advances in LLM capabilities, particularly in domains where correctness can be automatically verified (Shao et al., 2024; Liu and Zhang, 2025). In vision-language models, RL-based post-training has been applied to improve grounding (Shen et al., 2025), detection and classification (Liu et al., 2025), chart-based question answering (Sinha et al., 2025), and tool-augmented visual mathematical reasoning (Zhou et al., 2025)tasks that all admit deterministic verification via IoU, classification accuracy, or multiple-choice correctness. Open-ended dense captioning, where outputs are free-form and no ground-truth verifier exists, presents fundamentally harder challenge. The closest concurrent work, CapRL (Xing et al., 2025), constructs multiple-choice questions from strong VLM and treats derived accuracy as proxy reward. However, MCQ options are inherently limited in coverageany failure mode absent from the option set goes unpenalized, fundamentally capping evaluation by design. RubiCap sidesteps this limitation. By assembling diverse VLM committee to extract consensus-grounded rubrics, it produces open-ended evaluation criteria capable of surfacing failures that no fixed choice set could anticipate.

sections.title: 3 Framework
sections.content: RubiCap addresses the verification challenge that arises when applying RL to dense image captioning. Its key insight is to exploit sample-specific evaluation rubricsclear, interpretable rules that decompose caption quality into fine-grained, image-conditioned reward signals. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the framework operates in two stages: (1) Automated Rubric Synthesis (Sec. 3.2), which distills teacher consensus into per-image evaluation criteria; and (2) Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning (Sec. 3.3), which uses the derived criteria to optimize student captioning policy.

sections.title:
3.2 Automated Rubric Synthesis
Rather than relying on a single golden reference, we leverage the collective expertise of teacher models to
construct evaluation rubrics tailored to each training sample. Given an image x, we collect teacher captions
Cteacher(x) and generate a student caption cstudent from the current policy. An LLM rubric writer is then
prompted with the image, the student caption, and the teacher captions to produce targeted evaluation
criteria via three sequential steps:
sections.content: 1. Identify consensus aspects: The rubric writer extracts key descriptive elements on which the majority of teacher captions agree, conditioned on the image x. An elementwhether an object, attribute, spatial relationship, or contextual interpretationis treated as ground truth only if at least K/2 teachers describe it accurately, preventing any single noisy teacher from biasing the rubric. 2. Diagnose student deficiencies: The rubric writer performs comparative analysis between cstudent and the teacher consensus. Crucially, the rubric writer focuses on discriminative deficienciesflagging only the aspects the student failed to capture or misrepresented. This ensures the reward signal is non-redundant Identified failures are categorized by severity: (i) critical failures such as main-subject and targeted. misidentification or hallucination of major elements; (ii) important gaps such as missing secondary objects, imprecise attributes, or incorrect spatial logic; and (iii) minor polish issues such as phrasing clarity or fine-grained detail richness. 3. Formulate targeted rubrics. For each diagnosed deficiency, the rubric writer is asked to define binary, easy-to-check criterion rm paired with severity weight wm for minor, important, and critical failures, respectively. These weights can be viewed as hyperparameters; inspired by other rubric-based works (Shao et al., 2025; Gunjal et al., 2025), here we use {1.0, 2.0, 3.0}. Finally, every criterion must admit an unambiguous pass/fail judgment. The resulting sample-specific rubric set is: R(cid:0)x, cstudent, {cteacher }K k=1 (cid:1) = (cid:8)(rm, wm)(cid:9)M m=1, where each rm is human-readable binary statement. We present rubric example in Fig. 1, where the rubric writer asks the student model to identify text inscriptions on the cake; caption that correctly mentions 24 CARROT CAKE will be rewarded accordingly. By construction, is discriminativeevery criterion 4 targets real quality gap relative to teacher consensus. Moreover, it is image-conditioned, adapting to both the visual content of and the students current failure modes, rather than applying fixed, generic checklist. In practice, we develop the teacher committee with five VLMs, including Gemini 2.5 Pro (Comanici et al., 2025), GPT-5 (Singh et al., 2025), Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025), Gemma-3-27B-IT (Kamath et al., 2025), and Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025) to ensure diversity. We designate Gemini 2.5 Pro as our primary rubric writer and anonymize teacher identities within the synthesis prompts to prevent any stylistic bias. The complete prompt templates are provided in Appendix B.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We empirically evaluate the effectiveness of RubiCap by comparing the dense captions it generates against four reference points: those produced by (i) the student base model, (ii) diverse set of RL and SFT baselines, (iii) human-expert annotations, and (iv) proprietary model outputs. Our evaluation is organized around six core claims: C1. Greater Self-Improvement: RubiCap achieves higher win rate than supervised distillation and RL approaches that rely on NLP metrics or VLM judges, when all methods are compared against the same base model (Sec. 4.1). C2. Superior Caption Quality: RubiCap produces higher-quality captions than human-expert annotations, proprietary model captions, and outputs from concurrent work, CapRL (Xing et al., 2025) (Sec. 4.2). 5 C3. Knowledge Forgetting Mitigation: Unlike SFT-based methods, RubiCap substantially reduces catastrophic forgetting, better preserving the fine-tuned models pretrained capabilities (Sec. 4.3). C4. Advantage over Rubric-Augmented SFT: Even when identical rubrics are explicitly incorporated into SFT, RubiCap consistently produces higher-quality captions through better exploration across different model scales (Sec. 4.4). C5. Higher Information Density: RubiCap exhibits superior word efficiency, providing more salient content under strict length constraints. (Sec. 4.5). C6. Stronger Pretraining Utility: Using RubiCap-trained models as annotators for pretraining yields stronger VLMs compared to those annotated by proprietary systems (Sec. 4.6). Datasets. We construct two training datasets from different dense captioning sources: PixMoCap (Deitke et al., 2025) and DenseFusion-4V-100K (Li et al., 2024). From each, we randomly sample 50,000 images for training and reserve 500 images as held-out evaluation sets. These two datasets provide distinct forms of supervision that enable different evaluation objectives: (i) PixMoCap contains high-quality human-expert annotations where three annotators independently produce spoken descriptions for each image, which are subsequently refined and rewritten by an LLM to improve clarity and ensure stylistic consistency. (ii) DenseFusion-4V-100K contains dense captions generated by GPT-4V (OpenAI, 2023) and augmented with multiple perceptual attributes, representing strong outputs from proprietary VLM. Captions from both datasets serve as reference annotations for quality comparison throughout our experiments. Models and Baselines. We validate RubiCap through full-parameter fine-tuning on student models from the Qwen VLM family at multiple scales including Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025), Qwen2.5-VL-3BInstruct (Bai et al., 2025), and Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct (Wang et al., 2024). Caption rollouts are evaluated against each rubric criterion by Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, which serves as our LLM judge and provides binary satisfaction scores. Training configurations and compute details are provided in Appendix F. We compare RubiCap against five families of baselines: Base Model: the untuned student model, serving as reference point for measuring self-improvement. Supervised Distillation: supervised fine-tuning on captions from PixMoCap, DenseFusion, and strong frontier model (Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025)), all treated as ground-truth image descriptions. NLP Metricbased RL: reinforcement learning using ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004) as the reward signal, measuring lexical overlap between caption rollouts and reference captions. VLM Judgebased RL: two baseline variants from the RaR framework (Gunjal et al., 2025): DirectLikert and Reference-Likert. Both use Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct as VLM judge to assign quality scores on 010 scale. Reference-Likert evaluates quality relative to reference caption, whereas Direct-Likert scores captions without comparisons. The employed prompts are offered in Appendix C. CapRL-3B (Xing et al., 2025): method that leverages 75,000 well-curated images and multiple-choice questionbased caption quality scores as RL rewards. We compare at the 3B scale where it shares our student base model.

sections.title:
4.1 Comparison with Supervised Distillation and Other RL Objectives
Setup. We evaluate caption quality using CapArena (Cheng et al., 2025), a recent dense captioning bench-
mark that employs a strong VLM judge to compute pairwise win–loss rates between caption pairs. We adopt
the official CapArena evaluation protocol and use GPT-4.1 (OpenAI, 2024) as the judge. To the best of our
knowledge, CapArena’s annotated preferences exhibit the strongest alignment with human judgments among
existing automatic evaluation metrics. The full judging prompt is provided in Appendix D.
sections.content: In addition to CapArena, we report comprehensive suite of complementary metrics. These include modelbased measures including CAPTURE (Dong et al., 2024) and SPECS (Chen et al., 2025) (a specialized CLIP-based metric), as well as traditional lexical overlap metrics such as ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002). Detailed metric descriptions and complete results across model scales appear in Appendix G.

sections.title: PixMoCap
sections.content: DenseFusion-4V-100K Figure 2 Evaluation of RubiCap-7B models trained on PixMoCap and DenseFusion datasets. The top row shows results for PixMoCap, and the bottom row for DenseFusion. For each setting: Left: CapArena win rates against the base model across training steps, demonstrating consistent and strongest self-improvement over SFT variants and RL baselines. Middle: CapArena win rates against high-quality human-expert annotations (PixMoCap) and GPT-4V-augmented captions (DenseFusion), showing RubiCap surpasses professional and proprietary labeling systems. Right: Radar plots over 10 VLM benchmarks, validating RubiCap has better knowledge preservation of pretrained capabilities over supervised distillation. Average performance scores are provided in the boxes. Results. We begin by comparing RubiCap captions and baseline methods against those produced by the base model. This setting measures self-improvement, where the untuned model serves as the reference point at 50% win rate. The leftmost panels in Fig. 2 report results for 7B-scale models on the two datasets. RubiCap-7B consistently outperforms all baselines, including SFT variants trained on humanexpert annotations, GPT-4V-augmented captions, and synthetic captions from Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct. Specifically, RubiCap achieves +20.8% winrate improvement on PixMoCap and +14.4% improvement on DenseFusion. These yield the highest self-improvement among all compared methods. We further validate these gains at the 3B scale in Fig. 3 and report 2B-scale results in Appendix G. Across all settings and model sizes, RubiCap consistently achieves the highest win rates relative to base models. Moreover, we observe failure mode emerges from the Reference-Likert baseline. At the 3B and 2B scales, this method eventually induces self-praising behavior , obtaining high rewards without producing meaningful or detailed image descriptions 1. This behavior represents classic form of reward hacking , in which model discovers shortcut that maximizes the reward signal while entirely bypassing the intended generation objective (DeepMind, 2023; Amodei et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 3 (purple bar results), Reference-Likert results in the lowest win rates in the PixMoCap setting (achieving only 7.8% against the base model and 4.0% against human-expert annotations). These findings expose fundamental limitation of RL reward formulations: relying on holistic, unspecified 1Across virtually all test images, Reference-Likerttrained models produce captions such as: Certainly! Here is detailed description of the image: This image description is absolutely correct and complete. It is absolutely clear and detailed. 7 Figure 3 Performance of RubiCap-3B across PixMoCap and DenseFusion settings. Left: Results under PixMoCap. Right: Results under DenseFusion. In both settings, RubiCap-3B achieves the highest CapArena win rates among all compared methods. quality judgmentsa vibe checkcreates degenerate reward surfaces that admit shortcut generations and provide arbitrary training signals. In contrast, RubiCap overcomes this by grounding evaluation in explicit, sample-specific criteria, guiding the model toward detailed and accurate captioning.

sections.title:
4.2 Comparison with Human Annotations and Proprietary Models
Setup. Next, we conduct a more challenging evaluation by directly comparing RubiCap captions against
two strong references: expert-refined annotations from PixMoCap and GPT-4V-enriched outputs from
DenseFusion, representing professional human supervision and proprietary model outputs, respectively. We
also include a ranking-style comparison where we prompt GPT-4.1 to jointly rank captions from RubiCap
models, 72B, 32B frontiers, the base model, and the dataset reference captions. To prevent bias, model
identities are anonymized —the judge receives only the captions, with no information about their source. This
evaluation is inspired by the EXPERT metric (Kim et al., 2025), which decomposes caption quality into
sub-dimensions, and we rank models by their total scores. Our evaluation prompt is offered in Appendix D.
Finally, we include a head-to-head comparison against CapRL-3B (Xing et al., 2025), evaluating RubiCap at
the 3B and 2B scales.
sections.content: Results. Results for 7B-scale models are shown in the middle panels of Fig. 2. RubiCap consistently achieves the highest win rates across all compared methods. Relative to the base model, RubiCap-7B trained on PixMoCap and DenseFusion improves win rates by 13.4% and 8.4%, respectively. Importantly, RubiCapgenerated captions are preferred over both expert-refined human annotations and GPT-4Venriched outputswinning more than half of all pairwise comparisons. particularly revealing pattern emerges from the SFT baselines. Fine-tuning on human-annotated captions (PixMoCap) hurts base model performance (red line, middle panel), whereas distilling from same-family modelQwen2.5-VL-72B-Instructyields consistent gains (yellow line, middle panel). The difference points to distributional mismatch: even objectively high-quality captions can be misaligned with the students inherent distribution, making them poor fine-tuning targets. In contrast, RubiCap avoids this supervision limitation by optimizing against the students own rollouts. We present the blind ranking results in Fig. 4, where RubiCap-7B-PixMoCap earns the highest proportion of rank-1 assignmentssurpassing both 72B and 32B frontier models. Moreover, the sub-metric breakdown further reveals why: RubiCap achieves the lowest hallucination penalty and the strongest accuracy score among all models, while matching the 72B model on completeness and clarity. We demonstrate series of 8 Figure 4 Left: Rank Distribution per Model. Using the PixMoCap setting as an example, RubiCap achieves substantially higher proportion of rank-1 assignments, demonstrating consistently preferred captions despite its smaller scale. Right: Sub-metric Breakdown per Model. Scores across four evaluation dimensions show that RubiCap achieve lower hallucination penalty and stronger accuracy and clarity , while matching the 72B model overall. qualitative comparisons in Appendix I. Fig. 5 (left) shows CapArena win rates against CapRL-3B. RubiCap-3B achieves win rates of 62% on PixMoCap and 59% on DenseFusion, comfortably outperforming CapRL-3B. Notably, even RubiCap-2B remains competitive: models built on Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct achieve win rates of 54% and 51.4%, despite being trained on smaller size model and 25% less data than CapRL-3B. We also observe that CapRL3B exhibits similar self-praising behavior , frequently appending captions with meta-commentary such as: This detailed description should provide pure text model with sufficient information to answer any related questions about the image.

sections.title:
4.3 Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting
Setup. Continued supervised fine-tuning can risk in forgetting pretrained capabilities. To assess this, we test
fine-tuned models on 10 VLM benchmarks spanning four categories using VLMEvalKit (Duan et al., 2024):
(i) Visual Reasoning (GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019), BLINK (Fu et al., 2024)), (ii) Scientific Un-
derstanding (AI2D (Kembhavi et al., 2016)), (iii) OCR (RealWorldQA (xAI, 2024), OCRBench (Liu et al.,
2024), TextVQA (Singh et al., 2019), OCRVQA (Mishra et al., 2019)), and (iv) Document Extraction
(InfoVQA (Mathew et al., 2022), DocVQA (Mathew et al., 2020), ChartVQA (Masry et al., 2022)). We
report the average performance across all benchmarks as a measure of generalization retention.
sections.content: Results. We first focus on 7B-scale models, results are shown in the rightmost panels of Fig. 2, with 3Band 2B-scale results provided in Appendix H. Across all model scales, RubiCap consistently achieves the highest average performance over the 10 benchmarks, substantially outperforming SFT-based models, which exhibit the largest degradation. These results underscore the limitations of SFT on open-ended tasks, where it often impairs generalization, whereas RubiCap training more effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting.

sections.title:
4.4 Effectiveness beyond Rubric-Augmented SFT.
Setup. A natural question is whether RubiCap’s gains can be replicated by simply incorporating derived
rubrics into an SFT pipeline. To answer this, we construct a rubric-augmented SFT baseline that has access
to the same evaluation criteria as RubiCap but trains via supervised imitation rather than RL. Concretely,
for each image the student model first generates an initial caption and is then prompted with the full set
of rubrics to rewrite it such that all criteria are satisfied. The model is subsequently fine-tuned on these
rewritten captions via standard SFT. This baseline controls for teachers’ collective expertise and rubric
sections.content: 9 Figure 5 Left: Comparison with CapRL-3B in CapArena Evaluation. RubiCap variants achieve higher win rates across both 3B and 2B model sizes while using 25% less training data. Right: Comparison between rubricaugmented SFT and RubiCap across model scales. Even when provided identical rubrics, RubiCap consistently achieves higher win rates. Figure 6 RubiCap achieves word efficiency that surpasses larger models. The left panel shows results on PixMoCap; the right panel reports results on DenseFusion. RubiCap models consistently outperform both their same-size base models and larger counterparts under strict word-count constraints. This shows rubric-guided RL incurs more relevant and comprehensive captions. availability, allowing us to attribute remaining gap to the RL training paradigm itself. Experiments are conducted at the 3B and 7B scales using PixMoCap. Results. Fig. 5 (right) compares rubric-augmented SFT and RubiCap across both scales. First, RubiCap outperforms rubric-augmented SFT when evaluated against the base model. At 3B, RubiCap achieves win rate of 68.6%, compared to 64.0% for rubric-augmented SFT. This gap widens at 7B, where RubiCap reaches 70.8%, 5.0 percentage point advantage. Second, when compared to human-expert refined captions, this advantage persists: at 7B, RubiCap outperforms rubric-augmented SFT by 6.2 percentage points. These results indicate that conditioning on consensus-driven rubrics during supervised learning is insufficient to fully leverage their corrective potential . RubiCaps advantage stems not from rubric exposure alone, but from using rubrics as fine-grained reward signals within RL, enabling broader Table 1 VLM pretraining performance across 9 benchmarks when using captions generated by different annotators. Bold indicates best performance per benchmark; underline indicates the second best. RubiCap-annotated models outperform the GPT-4V baseline (a proprietary system) on average, using open models at 3B7B scale.

sections.content: AI2D ChartQA MathVista-CoT MathVista-Format MathVista-Solution MMBench (EN) MMMU MM-Vet ScienceQA

sections.title:
4.5 Caption Quality under Word Constraints
Setup. We further evaluate caption quality under explicit word-count constraints using CaptionQA (Yang
et al., 2025), a recent multiple-choice benchmark that assesses whether captions contain sufficient information
to answer curated MCQs. Higher CaptionQA scores indicate captions encode more relevant and comprehen-
sive information. We use Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct as the LLM evaluator and prompt the fine-tuned models
to generate captions under word limits ranging from 100 to 600 words. Experiments are conducted across
three scales: RubiCap-2B, RubiCap-3B, and RubiCap-7B, each compared against its corresponding untuned
checkpoint.
sections.content: Results. As shown in Fig. 6, RubiCap consistently outperforms its base model counterparts across all word-count limits and model scales. The gains are most pronounced in low-token regimes: under 100-word limit, RubiCap-7B improves CaptionQA by +12.01% over Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, and RubiCap3B improves by +9.53% over its 3B counterpart. Notably, performance gains are not strictly tied to model size: RubiCap-3B and RubiCap-2B both surpass the larger 7B base model under the same constraints. This cross-size advantage extends further: under 100300 word limits, RubiCap-7B outperforms the 32B model, and matches it under more relaxed 400600 word budgetsdelivering 32B-level caption quality at 7B inference cost. These results demonstrate: by covering multiple evaluation dimensions simultaneously, rubric-driven rewards train models to prioritize salient, information-dense content, producing higher-quality captions within tighter token budgets.

sections.title:
4.6 Enabling Stronger VLM Pretraining
Setup. Finally, we demonstrate the broader applicability of RubiCap as a scalable captioner for vision-
language model pretraining. We follow the LLaVA-NeXT training framework (Liu et al., 2024) but replace
the Stage 1.5 image captioning data with captions generated by RubiCap-3B and RubiCap-7B. Specifically,
we re-caption three large-scale datasets—COCO118K, BLIP558K, and CC3M—for a total of approximately
3.5 million images, while keeping all remaining instruction tuning data unchanged. The resulting VLM uses
CLIP-336px (Radford et al., 2021) as the vision encoder and Qwen2-7B (Bai et al., 2025) as the LLM backbone.
The performance is evaluated on nine benchmarks: AI2D (Kembhavi et al., 2016), ChartQA (Masry et al.,
2022), MathVista (3 variants) (Lu et al., 2023), MMBench (EN) (Liu et al., 2024), MMMU (Yue et al., 2024),
MM-Vet (Yu et al., 2023), ScienceQA (Lu et al., 2022). As a direct baseline, we train an identical model
under the same pipeline but with GPT-4V-generated captions.
sections.content: 11 Results. Table 1 reports performance across 9 VLM benchmarks. All three RubiCap-annotated models outperform the GPT-4V baseline on average, yielding relative improvement of 3.42%. Remarkably, even our compact 3B-scale RubiCap models produce pretraining captions that yield stronger pretrained VLMs than those annotated by GPT-4V, matching or exceeding proprietary model quality at fraction of the cost. These results establish RubiCap-trained models as practical, scalable alternative to proprietary systems for vision-language pretraining.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We present RubiCap, framework that addresses the verification bottleneck in RL-based dense captioning by deriving synthetic, sample-specific rubrics as fine-grained reward signals. Extensive evaluations confirm RubiCaps effectiveness across multiple axes: it achieves the highest win rates in GPT-4.1-judged pairwise comparisons against all baselines, surpasses human-expert annotations and GPT-4V-augmented outputs at the 7B scale, and preserves broad pretrained capabilities far more effectively than supervised distillation. In blind ranking evaluation, RubiCap-7B obtains the highest proportion of rank-1 assignments, outperforming 72B and 32B frontiers. Under fixed word budgets, RubiCap produces more information-dense captions, enabling 7B model to match 32B-scale performancedelivering frontier-level caption quality at fraction of the inference cost. Finally, compact RubiCap-3B serves as an effective pretraining annotators, yielding stronger VLMs than those built on proprietary GPT-4V captions.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: Appendix Roadmap. Our appendix is structured as follows. We begin with the prompts underlying our framework: Appendix presents those used to synthesize rubrics and apply them via an LLM judge for RL rewards, Appendix covers the prompts used by baseline methods with VLM-as-a-judge, and Appendix details the prompts for assessing caption quality in the CapArena benchmark and the blind ranking experiment. Appendix then summarizes our full framework in pseudocode, and Appendix documents the experimental setup and training configurations. We next turn to results: Appendix reports full evaluation tables across 7B, 3B, and 2B models on comprehensive captioning metrics, while Appendix presents VLM performance for measuring knowledge retention. Finally, Appendix offers qualitative comparisons, showcasing sampled captions from our model alongside those from the base model and Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct.

sections.title: B Rubric Synthesis and Judging Prompts
sections.content: We first provide the prompts used to synthesize targeted rubrics from committee of candidate captions: the system prompt in Prompt 1 and the user prompt in Prompt 2. Both prompts are sent to the rubric writer, which is instructed to return structured JSON outputs. Each rubric item contains the criterion, evaluation rule, severity weight, and supporting justification traced back to teacher consensus, providing an interpretable audit trail for every scoring decision. Our framework is agnostic to the specific models in the teacher committee; any set of diverse captioners can serve this role. In our experiments, we instantiate the committee with five VLMs spanning different model families to maximize descriptive diversity . Once rubrics are synthesized, the entire RL training loop requires only lightweight LLM judge (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, 7B open-source model), with no further dependence on proprietary systems. Notably, the committee is invoked only once per image during rubric constructionit is an offline preprocessing step (a one-time investment), not recurring labeling cost. You are an expert evaluator generating highly discriminative rubrics to assess image caption quality. Prompt 1 System Prompt for Rubric Writer ## Task Identify the most discriminative criteria that separate excellent captions from weak or flawed ones. Focus (cid:44) on subtle but decisive quality differences that generic rubrics typically miss, while covering all (cid:44) critical dimensions of captioning performance. ## Rules you MUST follow: 1. Discriminative Power (Highest Priority) - **Only** include criteria where the student model actually **fails** relative to teacher majority. Do NOT (cid:44) create rubrics for aspects the student model already handles correctly. - Each rubric MUST meaningfully distinguish the weak student caption from teacher-consensus level (cid:44) performance. 2. Teacher Consensus as Ground Truth - Ground truth = majority agreement among the five teacher models. - visual element, relationship, or interpretation is considered correct only if >= 2 teachers describe it (cid:44) accurately. 3. Weighting by Severity - 3.0: Critical failures (main subject misidentification, hallucination of major elements, missing (cid:44) essential relationships) - 2.0: Important but non-critical (secondary objects, spatial/contextual accuracy, attribute precision) - 1.0: Minor polish (style fluency, phrasing clarity, minor detail richness) 4. Binary & Verifiable - Every criterion must have clear, objective pass/fail rule that can be verified by directly comparing (cid:44) the student caption against teacher consensus. 5. Quality over Quantity - Prefer extremely important and sharp rubrics over many many generic ones. 16 ## Output Requirements: IMPORTANT: Return valid JSON object only, enclosed in triple backticks (json). Do not include any (cid:44) additional text, explanations, or comments outside the JSON. Escape all quotes within string values (cid:44) using backslash ("). Do not use single quotes or unescaped double quotes within JSON string values: **JSON Structure:** { "rubrics": [ { "criterion": "Clear, specific criterion (e.g., Identifies the red bicycle in foreground)", "description": "Detailed explanation of what this measures and why it matters", "evaluation_rule": "Concrete rule with clear pass/fail condition", "weight": 1.02.03.0, "justification": "Explain the gap: what you see in the image, what teachers captured, what weak model (cid:44) missed/got wrong", "student_already_met": "True or False - Whether the weak model already satisfies this criterion", "reference_teachers": ["List of teacher model names that correctly satisfied this criterion"], "teacher_consensus": "Description of what the majority of reliable teachers agree on for this element" } ] } {{IMAGE}} **Weak Model Output:** {{WEAK_STUDENT_CAPTION}} **Teacher Model Outputs:** 1. **Model 1:** {{CANDIDATE_1}} 2. **Model 2:** {{CANDIDATE_2}} 3. **Model 3:** {{CANDIDATE_3}} 4. **Model 4:** {{CANDIDATE_4}} 5. **Model 5:** {{CANDIDATE_5}} Prompt 2 User Prompt for Rubric Writer **Task:** 1. Carefully examine the image and identify all important visual elements. 2. Determine teacher consensus (what the majority of the five teacher models describe correctly). 3. Evaluate the weak models caption across all important dimensions of caption quality: accuracy, (cid:44) completeness, clarity, detail, relationships, and contextual interpretation. 4. For each dimension you choose to include, create one targeted binary rubric item with appropriate weight (cid:44) (1.0-3.0) using the required JSON structure. 5. Do NOT create rubrics for aspects the weak model already handles correctly. Next, we present the prompt for the LLM judge that provides RL rewards. Prompt 3 instructs the judge to evaluate generated caption rollouts against given rubric criterion and its evaluation rule, assigning binary satisfaction score. The returned JSON includes reasoning trace alongside the score. You are Quality Assurance Auditor for image captioning. Your task is to validate if generated caption (cid:44) adheres to specific criterion based on meaning and intent, not just keyword matching. Prompt 3 User Prompt for RubiCaps Reward Provider CRITERION TO EVALUATE (THE ONLY THING YOU USE TO CONSIDER): - Criteria name: {criterion} - Criteria description: {description} - Evaluation rule: {evaluation_rule} GENERATED CAPTION TO EVALUATE: {generated_caption} EVALUATION INSTRUCTIONS: 1. **Analyze Requirements:** Break down the criteria description and its evaluation rule to understand the (cid:44) core facts or concepts required. 2. **Check Semantic Equivalence:** Analyze the generated caption. Check if the required concepts are (cid:44) present, even if phrased differently. - Synonyms are acceptable (e.g., "automobile" instead of "car"). - Structural changes are acceptable (e.g., "The man holds cup" vs "A cup is held by the man"). 3. **Assign Score:** - Score 1 (Pass): The caption conveys the correct *meaning* of the rule. It includes the necessary (cid:44) details, regardless of phrasing. - Score 0 (Fail): The caption explicitly misses key information, contradicts the facts, or hallucinates (cid:44) details not found in the rule. RESPONSE FORMAT: json {{ "reasoning": "<detailed explanation of the score, why the caption does or does not fully meet the given (cid:44) criterion and its evaluation rule>", "score": <0 or 1> }} IMPORTANT: Return valid JSON object only, enclosed in triple backticks (json). Do not include any (cid:44) additional text, explanations, or comments outside the JSON. Escape all quotes within string values (cid:44) using backslash ("). Do not use single quotes or unescaped double quotes within JSON string values.

sections.title: C Baseline Judging Prompts
sections.content: We provide the prompts for two baseline reward methods: Direct-Likert and Reference-Likert. Both employ VLM-as-a-judge approach, using Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct as the judge. Prompts 4 and 5 instruct the judge to rate caption quality on scale from 0 to 10. In Prompt 5, reference caption is additionally provided to guide the evaluation. Prompt 4 User Prompt for VLM Judge in Direct-Likert Method You are an expert image caption evaluator. Generated Caption: {generated_caption} Evaluate the generated caption on these criteria: 1. Accuracy: Does it correctly describe whats in the image? 2. Completeness: Does it cover the important details? 3. Quality: Is it well-written and coherent? Provide score from 0 to 10, where: - 0-3: Poor quality, major inaccuracies or missing key information - 4-6: Adequate, captures main elements but lacks detail or has minor issues - 7-8: Good, accurate and detailed description - 9-10: Excellent, comprehensive and high-quality caption Respond with ONLY single number from 0 to 10, nothing else. Prompt 5 User Prompt for VLM Judge in Reference-Likert Method You are an expert image caption evaluator. Compare the generated caption with the reference caption for the (cid:44) given image. Reference Caption: {reference_caption} Generated Caption: {generated_caption} Evaluate the generated caption on these criteria: 1. Accuracy: Does it correctly describe whats in the image? 2. Completeness: Does it cover the important details? 3. Quality: Is it well-written and coherent? Provide score from 0 to 10, where: - 0-3: Poor quality, major inaccuracies or missing key information - 4-6: Adequate, captures main elements but lacks detail or has minor issues - 7-8: Good, accurate and detailed description - 9-10: Excellent, comprehensive and high-quality caption 18 Respond with ONLY single number from 0 to 10, nothing else.

sections.title: D Evaluation Prompts
sections.content: We provide the evaluation prompts used throughout our experiments. Prompt 6, adopted from CapArena (Cheng et al., 2025), is used to compute pairwise win-lose rates as judged by GPT-4.1. Caption assignments are randomized to mitigate positional bias, and the judge returns detailed justification along with the winner decision. Prompt 7 instructs the evaluator to blindly rank captions and assess quality along four dimensions: accuracy, completeness, clarity, and hallucination penalty. We evaluate five captions: those generated by RubiCap, Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct (the student base model), and an expert-annotated caption from PixMoCap. The evaluator returns per-dimension scores and an overall ranking. Given an image and two candidate captions, you are required to determine which of the two captions is (cid:44) better. Prompt 6 Used Prompt for WinLose Rate in CapArena Below are some guidelines for your reference: ### What to evaluate 1. **Precision**: The caption should accurately correspond to the content of the image, providing precise (cid:44) information about it. Common examples of imprecision include errors in color, quantity, spatial (cid:44) relationships, or the posture of people. 2. **Informativeness**: Salient information in the image should be reflected in the caption. Since it is (cid:44) impossible to include every detail, you will need to subjectively judge which aspects of the image are (cid:44) important. For instance, describing an otter as "a small animal" is precise, but it is less informative (cid:44) than specifying "an otter". 3. **Hallucination**: Captions that include descriptions of objects or elements that are clearly absent (cid:44) from the image should be significantly penalized. 4. **Attention to detail**: Annotators should pay close attention to the details in the image to (cid:44) distinguish the quality of the descriptions. 5. **Assistive description**: Imagine visually impaired person asking you to describe the image for them. (cid:44) How would you convey the image to them? 6. **Reverse thinking**: What image does the caption lead us to imagine? Does the caption effectively lead (cid:44) you to imagine the intended image? 7. **Ties are acceptable**: If you find it genuinely difficult to determine which caption is better (e.g., (cid:44) both captions are excellent), marking tie is acceptable. While the above guidelines provide (cid:44) framework, they cannot cover all possible cases. Therefore, we encourage you to make **subjective (cid:44) judgments** based on the specific circumstances and your own reasoning about which caption is better. ### What to ignore When comparing the captions, **do NOT consider differences in**: - Writing style or phrasing - **Caption length** - Grammatical variations Caption A: {caption_a} Caption B: {caption_b} Format your response in JSON format. RESPONSE FORMAT: json {{ "reason": "<detailed explanation of the judgment>", "judgment": "<A, B, or, Tie>" }} Prompt 7 User Prompt to rank captions and assess caption quality by breaking down into four dimentions: accuracy, completeness, clarity, and hallucination penalty. You are an expert image captioning evaluator. Given the image above and the 5 captions below, rigorously (cid:44) assess each caption. ## Scoring Score each caption on four dimensions (integers 0-10): 1. accuracy - Are the described objects, actions, text, colors, and spatial relationships factually correct (cid:44) for this image? Penalize for any wrong attribute, misidentified object, or incorrect action. 2. completeness - Does the caption cover all visually significant elements (main subjects, notable actions, (cid:44) background context, on-screen text if present)? Penalize for missing key details. 3. clarity - Is the caption well-written, specific, grammatically correct, and unambiguous? Penalize for (cid:44) vague language or redundancy. 4. hallucination_penalty - Does the caption assert things NOT visible in the image? 0 = zero hallucination; (cid:44) 10 = pervasive fabrication. Be strict: even plausible but unverifiable claims count as mild (cid:44) hallucination (2-4). This score is applied as penalty. Compute: total_score = (accuracy + completeness + clarity) / 3.0 - hallucination_penalty 1.5 ## Captions to Evaluate {captions_text} ## Output Format Respond ONLY with single valid JSON object - no markdown fences, no extra text. { } "assessments": { "Caption A": { "justification": "<2-3 sentences citing specific visual evidence from the image>", "accuracy": <int 0-10>, "completeness": <int 0-10>, "clarity": <int 0-10>, "hallucination_penalty": <int 0-10>, "total_score": <float> }, ... "Caption E": { ... } }, "ranking": ["Caption X", "Caption X", "Caption X", "Caption X", "Caption X"] The "ranking" list must contain all 5 caption labels ordered from best (index 0) to worst (index 4), sorted (cid:44) strictly by total_score descending.

sections.title: E Algorithm Overview
sections.content: We summarize the two stages of RubiCap as unified pseudocode in Algorithm 1. The prompt used in Stage 1 (Sec. 3.2) is general, extensible template that any user can steer the rubrics focus toward specific areas of weakness by design.

sections.title: F Training Details
sections.content: We detail the training configurations and computational resources used in our experiments. We sample 50,000 images from PixMoCap 2 and DenseFusion 3, holding out 500 for evaluation and using the remainder for training. We use the Qwen VLM family at different scales as our base student models and treat Qwen2.52https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/pixmo-cap 3https://huggingface.co/datasets/BAAI/DenseFusion-1M 20 Algorithm 1 RubiCap Require: Image dataset D, student policy πθs Judge, number of rollouts , clipping threshold ϵ, severity weights {1, 2, 3}. , teacher committee = {Tk}K k=1 , LLM Rubric Writer, LLM 1: {Stage 1: Automated Rubric Synthesis (Sec. 3.2)} 2: for each image do 3: Generate teacher captions: Cteacher(x) = {cteacher 4: Generate student caption: cstudent πθs ( x) 5: 6: }K k=1 Step 1: Extract consensus elements agreed upon by K/2 teachers conditioned on Step 2: Diagnose discriminative student deficiencies by comparing cstudent against teacher consensus; categorize by severity (critical, important, minor) Step 3: Formulate targeted rubrics: R(x, cstudent, Cteacher(x)) = {(rm, wm)}M binary criterion with severity weight wm , where each rm is m=1 where cteacher Tk(x) 7: 15: 16: 17: 8: end for 9: {Stage 2: Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning (Sec. 3.3)} 10: while not converged do 11: 12: 13: 14: Sample batch of images Sample rollouts: {cstudent for each rollout {1, . . . , } do LLM Judge evaluates each criterion: ˆyi,m {0, 1} for all rm R(x) Compute reward: Gi = }N i=1 πθs( x) (cid:80)M m=1 wmˆyi,m (cid:80)M m=1 wm end for Estimate advantages: Ai = Update θs by minimizing LGRPO(θs) Gimean({Gj }N std({Gj }N j=1) j=1) 18: 19: end while 7B-Instruct (LLM-only) as the reward provider to evaluate caption rollouts against the synthesized rubrics. All experiments are conducted on 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. We optimize the student policy using GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) with the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 1 105 with cosine scheduling and warmup ratio of 0.01, maximum completion length of 1,024 tokens, and = 4 generations per image. Training runs for 1 epoch. All the RL baseline methods employ the same training configuration, except for the reward design. During rollout generation, we prompt the student with: You are an expert at describing images in detail. Provide comprehensive, accurate captions that describe the main subjects, their actions, the setting, and important visual details in the image. For the supervised fine-tuning baseline, we use learning rate of 1 105 and train for 1 epoch. We explore diverse supervision signals for SFT, including expert-refined annotations from PixMoCap, GPT-4Vaugmented captions from DenseFusion, and distilled captions from the Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct frontier model.

sections.title: G Captioning Results
sections.content: In addition to CaptionQA (Yang et al., 2025) and the blind ranking evaluation inspired by EXPERT (Kim et al., 2025) used in the main paper, we report full results across all captioning evaluation metrics considered. Each of them has been shown to align well with human preference. We describe each metric below, then present complete results in Table 2 (RubiCap-7B), Table 3 (RubiCap-3B), and Table 4 (RubiCap-2B). They are: CapArena Win Rate (Cheng et al., 2025): pairwise preference-based metric in which strong VLM judge compares two captions and selects the better one. The win rate measures how often the models caption is preferred over reference across pairwise comparisons. We use GPT-4.1 as the judge. CAPTURE (Dong et al., 2024): reference-based metric that uses T5-based parser to extract structured visual elements (e.g., objects, attributes, and relations) from both the candidate and ground-truth 21 Table 2 Full captioning evaluation results for RubiCap-7B. Precision and recall are then computed by matching elements via exact, synonym, and soft (cosine similarity-based) methods. CAPTURE has shown higher consistency with expert judgments than other rule-based caption metrics. SPECS (Chen et al., 2025): reference-free representational similarity metric tailored to long image captioning. SPECS builds on LongCLIP (Zhang et al., 2024) with training objective that decomposes captions into fine-grained detail units and applies contrastive, positive, and negative losses to reward accurate visual details while penalizing inaccuracies. ROUGE-L (Lin, 2004): text overlap metric that measures the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) between the generated and reference captions. METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005): reference-based metric that goes beyond exact word matching by incorporating synonyms, stemming, and paraphrase matching. BLEU-4 (Papineni et al., 2002): precision-based metric that counts how many 4-gram sequences in the generated caption also appear in the reference, with brevity penalty for short outputs. Results. RubiCap consistently outperforms all RL baseline methods across both datasets and all model scales (see Tables 2, 3, and 4). The largest gains appear at 7B, with average improvements of +6.05 on PixMoCap and +4.80 on DenseFusion, and CapArena win rates reaching 70.80% against the base model (+20.80). At 3B, RubiCap maintains clear gains (+4.62 and +2.68 on average) while substantially outperforming CapRL-3B, which collapses to SPECS score of 0.00 on both datasets. Notably, the Reference-Likert baseline fails at 3B and 2B (averaging 7.33 and 11.08), exhibiting severe self-praising behavior and reward hackinga failure mode that RubiCap avoids entirely.
